english: 1. num. figure: 45. — 2. in W. gen. for byi (byi). — 3. bi (bi) and biu (bi'u) Pur. for bya (bya) bird, fowl, hen.
in compounds big (big), hole W. for bú-ga (bu ga), cf. big-pa (big pa); bi-gaṅ-ċaṅ (bi gang cang) having holes.
Ssk., lhai bzó-bo (lha'i bzo bo) the smith of the gods, the Brahman-Buddhist Vulcan Dzl., Glr.
(Ssk. word for poison) n. of certain medicinal plants, e.g. bi-ṣa-dkar (bi Sha dkar) Polygonatum, in Lh.
Cs. vitriol; Sch. potash, garlic-ashes; mentioned in S.g. as a caustic.
Ld. mouth-piece of a hautboy, hautboy reed.

Momordica monadelpha, a cucurbitaceous plant with a red fruit Wdn., along with ka-bed (ka bed); the fashion of Indian poets to compare red lips with the bimpa fruit, has been adopted also by the Tibetans, Gyatch. p. ༩༩; transl. p. 108; so also Pth.: mču-sgrós bim-pa ltá-bur mdzes (mchu sgros bim pa lta bur mdzes) (where Sch. gives the signification of peach, on which the name possibly may have been transferred, although 'lips of the shape of a peach-tree leaf' seem to be rather a strange fancy).
Ssk. बिल्व, Hind. bilb, bél, Aegle marmelos, tree with a nourishing and wholesome fruit; the word seems to have been transferred also to the cocoa-nut.

I. sbst., resp. sras (sras), 1. son, common in B. and C.; *čé-bu* (*che bu*) W., the eldest son. — 2. child, bu btsá-ba (bu btsa ba) the bringing forth of children, children being born Dzl.; bu máṅ-bar gyúr-ba (bu mang bar gyur ba) to get many children; bu mi ɣsós-pa (bu mi gsos pa) not being able to keep a child alive Dom.; esp. in reference to the mother: ma-bu (ma bu), mother and children; also transferred on animals: rta ma brgya bu (rta ma brgya bu) brgya (brgya) a hundred mares with as many foals Dzl.; the word is moreover used in many other instances, e.g. with regard to letters which in writing are placed under other letters, in reference to principal beams and smaller cross-beams, to capital and interest; also as a friendly address of a teacher to his hearers Mil. — The fem. bú-mo (bu mo) v. below. Comp. bu-krid (bu krid) (or pu-tri (pu tri)?) a fem. noun proper. — bu-grógs (bu grogs) Cs. step-brother, foster-brother. — bu-rgyúd (bu rgyud) offspring, issue, progeny, generation Tar. 168. 11. — bu-dód (bu dod) foster-child, adopted son, ṅéd-kyi bu-dód mdzod (nged kyi bu dod mdzod) deign to be adopted by us Mil. — bu-ɣdúṅ (bu gdung) a small cross-beam Mil. — bu-nád (bu nad) child-bed, bu-nád log (bu nad log) the child-bed terminates unfavourably Pth. — bu-snód (bu snod) uterus, womb Med. — bú-po (bu po) male child, son Dzl. — bu-prug (bu phrug) children. — bú-mo (bu mo), vulg. also bó-mo (bo mo) 1. daughter, frq. 2. girl, ṅa bú-moi dús-na yin-te (nga bu mo'i dus na yin te) when I was still a girl Glr. — kyeu (khye'u) daṅ (dang) bú-mo (bu mo) lads and lasses Dzl.; maiden, virgin; bú-mo ytsáṅ-ma (bu mo gtsang ma), ɣsár-ma (gsar ma), ɣsár-pa (gsar pa) a girl that is still in a virgin state. 3. young woman Dzl.; W. gen. for bud-méd (bud med), frq. — bu-smád (bu smad), Cs. also bu-mád (bu mad) family, children, nearest relations Mil. and elsewh. — bu-tsá (bu tsha) (Dzl. ed. Sch. also bu-tsá (bu tsha)) 1. children's children Thgy.; family = bu-smád (bu smad) 2. W., son, gen. for bu (bu); boy, *bú-tsa daṅ bó-mo* (*bu tsha dang bo mo*). — bu-tsáb (bu tshab) Cs. = bu-dod (bu dod). — bu-tsás ‚brél-ba (bu tshas 'brel ba) Glr. (acc. to the context) to cohabit. — bu-sriṅ (bu sring) brother and sister. — bu-slób (bu slob) scholar, disciple, follower of a clerical teacher, opp. to nyá-ma (nya ma) hearer, who still continues in his secular calling. — II. num. figure: 75.

1. in compounds bug (bug), hole, opening, orifice, aperture, bá-spui bú-ga (ba spu'i bu ga) pore, passage of perspiration Dzl.; sna-búg (sna bug) nostril; bú-ga dgu(-po) (bu ga dgu po) the nine orifices of the body (eyes, ears, nostrils, mouth, urethra, anus); tsáṅs-pai bú-ga (tshangs pa'i bu ga) and perh. also yíd-jug bú-ga (yid 'jug bu ga) Med., appears to be = mtsog-ma (mtshog ma) the fontanel or vacancy in the infant cranium, with which various fables are connected; cavity, vessel, (anatom.), also veins Med. — 2. symbol. num.: 9.
